title: Persistence of asymptotic variance under transport: from hyperfluctuation to stealthy hyperuniformity
abstract: We introduce $p$-uniformity to characterize the scaling of density fluctuations in spatial random systems in $\mathbb{R}^d$, ranging from hyperfluctuation to stealthy hyperuniformity. Our central theorem establishes sufficient conditions to preserve $p$-uniformity under transport. The first condition, a finite $(d+p)$-th moment of the transport distance, allows for a Taylor expansion of the transport. The second condition controls the corresponding terms. We thus solve a previously stated open problem; indeed we extend it, since our result applies to a general $p$-uniform source in any dimension, and the source and transport may be dependent. As an application, we construct new classes of point processes that are isotropic and $p$-uniform with arbitrarily high $p$, and that can be simulated in linear time. We conclude with an outlook on a converse statement.
summary: This paper provides a mathematical framework to understand how density fluctuations, ranging from hyperfluctuation to stealthy hyperuniformity, behave under random mass transport. It solves an open problem regarding the persistence of these fluctuations in dimensions d >= 3 and introduces new, efficiently simulatable point processes.
main_problem: Determining the conditions under which the scaling of density fluctuations (p-uniformity) is preserved when a random measure undergoes a random transport process.
main_result: The authors establish a persistence theorem stating that p-uniformity is preserved under transport if the transport distance has a finite (d+p)-th moment and certain expansion terms are controlled.
method: The study uses Fourier analysis of the Bartlett spectral measure, Taylor expansions of the transport kernel, and moment conditions on transport distance.
model_or_system: The model involves stationary random complex measures on R^d, including point processes and mass-redistribution (transport) kernels that move mass from a source to a destination.
key_observables: p-uniformity exponent, asymptotic variance, Bartlett spectral measure, and structure factor.
important_parameters: Spatial dimension (d), hyperuniformity exponent (p), and transport distance moments.
main_assumptions: The measures are assumed to be stationary (translation-invariant) and locally square-integrable; the expansion requires smoothness of test functions.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the construction of a random fair tiling and a resulting point process, demonstrating an isotropic process with a very high scaling exponent.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining p-uniformity and transport kernels, develops a Taylor expansion for the destination measure, proves the main persistence theorem, and concludes with new point process constructions and an outlook on the converse statement.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Illustration of our construction of an isotropic point process that is hyperuniform with an exceptionally large scaling exponent. In black and fading out from left to right, we show a realization of a random fair tiling, i.e., a random division of space into cells of equal volume; specifically, a realization of a fair STIT; see Subsection 5.1. To obtain the point process shown in blue, we concentrate the mass that is uniformly distributed over each cell into n points while preserving the first p moments, i.e., satisfying (1.8).
